examples.title: State Transition Algorithm for Concurrent Activities
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how event-driven state transitions work in the Concurrent Model using a formal state machine approach
examples.code:
BEGIN
 CONSTANTS
 STATE_INACTIVE = 0
 STATE_ACTIVE = 1
 STATE_COMPLETED = 2
 
 FUNCTION Transition(state, event)
 // Transition function δ: S × E → S
 IF state = STATE_INACTIVE AND event = "start" THEN
 RETURN STATE_ACTIVE
 ELSE IF state = STATE_ACTIVE AND event = "complete" THEN
 RETURN STATE_COMPLETED
 ELSE IF state = STATE_ACTIVE AND event = "block" THEN
 RETURN STATE_INACTIVE
 ELSE IF state = STATE_COMPLETED AND event = "rework" THEN
 RETURN STATE_ACTIVE
 ELSE
 RETURN state // No transition for undefined pairs
 END IF
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Example: Process activity through states
 currentState = STATE_INACTIVE
 events = ["start", "block", "start", "complete"]
 
 FOR EACH event IN events DO
 currentState = Transition(currentState, event)
 PRINT "Event: " + event + " → State: " + currentState
 END FOR
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode implements the formal state machine definition M = (S, E, δ, s₀) where S = {inactive, active, completed} and δ is the transition function. It demonstrates how events trigger state transitions, which is the fundamental mechanism of the Concurrent Model.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of events
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) constant space for state variables

examples.title: Join Synchronization Implementation
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how join synchronization coordinates dependent concurrent activities, ensuring one activity waits for another to complete
examples.code:
BEGIN
 // Shared variables
 BOOLEAN moduleA_completed = FALSE
 BOOLEAN moduleB_completed = FALSE
 BOOLEAN integration_ready = FALSE
 
 // Thread/Team A: Produces Module A
 THREAD TeamA()
 PRINT "Team A: Working on Module A"
 // Work for 10 days
 MODULE_A = produceModuleA()
 moduleA_completed = TRUE
 PRINT "Team A: Module A completed"
 END THREAD
 
 // Thread/Team B: Produces Module B
 THREAD TeamB()
 PRINT "Team B: Working on Module B"
 // Work for 15 days
 MODULE_B = produceModuleB()
 moduleB_completed = TRUE
 PRINT "Team B: Module B completed"
 END THREAD
 
 // Thread/Integration: Joins on both modules
 THREAD IntegrationTeam()
 // Join synchronization: wait until both modules ready
 WHILE NOT (moduleA_completed AND moduleB_completed) DO
 PRINT "Integration: Waiting for modules..."
 SLEEP(1) // Wait 1 time unit
 END WHILE
 
 PRINT "Integration: Both modules ready, starting integration"
 INTEGRATION_RESULT = integrate(MODULE_A, MODULE_B)
 PRINT "Integration: Complete"
 END THREAD
 
 // Start concurrent execution
 PARALLEL_EXECUTE(TeamA, TeamB, IntegrationTeam)
END
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates join synchronization where the IntegrationTeam must wait (join) until both TeamA and TeamB complete their modules. This ensures that dependent activities only start when their prerequisites are ready, which is essential for managing dependencies in concurrent development.

examples.title: Event-Driven Concurrent Process Controller
examples.description: A process controller that manages multiple concurrent activities by listening for events and triggering appropriate state transitions
examples.code:
BEGIN
 // Activity registry
 ARRAY activities = [
 {name: "Requirements", state: "inactive", dependencies: []},
 {name: "Design", state: "inactive", dependencies: ["Requirements"]},
 {name: "Implementation", state: "inactive", dependencies: ["Design"]},
 {name: "Testing", state: "inactive", dependencies: ["Implementation"]}
 ]
 
 // Event queue
 QUEUE eventQueue
 
 FUNCTION processEvent(event)
 // Find activity affected by event
 activity = findActivity(event.activityName)
 oldState = activity.state
 
 // Apply transition function δ
 newState = applyTransition(activity.state, event.type)
 activity.state = newState
 
 PRINT event.activityName + ": " + oldState + " → " + newState + " (triggered by " + event.type + ")"
 
 // Check if dependencies now satisfied for dependent activities
 FOR EACH dependent IN activities DO
 IF activity.name IN dependent.dependencies THEN
 IF allDependenciesSatisfied(dependent) THEN
 triggerEvent(dependent.name, "start")
 END IF
 END IF
 END FOR
 END FUNCTION
 
 FUNCTION allDependenciesSatisfied(activity)
 FOR EACH depName IN activity.dependencies DO
 depActivity = findActivity(depName)
 IF depActivity.state ≠ "completed" THEN
 RETURN FALSE
 END IF
 END FOR
 RETURN TRUE
 END FUNCTION
 
 // Simulate event processing
 triggerEvent("Requirements", "start")
 // After some work completes
 triggerEvent("Requirements", "complete")
 triggerEvent("Design", "start")
 // And so on...
END
examples.explanation: This controller implements the core Concurrent Model principles: activities have states, transitions occur based on events, and dependencies between activities are tracked. When an activity completes, it automatically triggers dependent activities if all their dependencies are satisfied, demonstrating event-driven coordination.
examples.timeComplexity: O(a × d) where a = number of activities, d = max dependencies
